lessons.title: Deprecated and Removed in C++20
lessons.motivation: When you migrate a codebase to C++20, the compiler suddenly warns you about things you've been writing for years: `std::result_of`, `std::is_pod`, using `std::iterator` as a base class — all deprecated. And some old habits like `std::random_shuffle` and `std::binary_function` simply won't compile at all. Knowing what's deprecated, what's removed, and what replaced them is the essential prerequisite for a smooth migration.

examples.title: Modern replacements for deprecated utilities
examples.description: Demonstrates `std::invoke_result_t` instead of `std::result_of`, a composed predicate instead of `std::is_pod`, `std::shuffle` instead of `std::random_shuffle`, and `std::allocator_traits` instead of direct `allocator::construct`/`destroy` calls.
examples.expectedOutput:
result type is double: 1
Plain is pod-like: 1
Virtual is pod-like: 0
shuffled: 5 1 3 4 2 
constructed: 42

examples.title: volatile: what's deprecated and what to use instead
examples.description: Compound assignments on `volatile` variables are deprecated in C++20. For shared state between threads, use `std::atomic`. Also shows the modern way to define iterator associated types without inheriting from `std::iterator`.

lessons.title: C++20 in Context: What It Changed and the Path to C++23/26
lessons.motivation: You've completed the course. But individual features studied one by one don't yet form a complete picture. What did C++20 actually change structurally in the language? How do these features relate to what came later in C++23 and C++26? Where can you get compiler support right now? This lesson assembles everything into a single map and shows where to go next.
lessons.theory:
## What C++20 Changed Structurally

C++20 is the largest update to the standard since C++11. Not just a set of individual features — it shifted paradigms in several fundamental areas.

### The Four Flagship Features

| Feature | What it changes |
|---------|----------------|
| **Concepts** | Template programming stops being "guess the error from a wall of text." Requirements on types are expressed explicitly; compiler messages become readable. |
| **Ranges and Views** | Algorithms no longer require iterator pairs. Lazy transformation chains read like SQL. |
| **Coroutines** | Async code without callbacks and state-machine boilerplate. The base mechanism; the library on top came in C++23. |
| **Modules** | An alternative to `#include`: pre-compilation, isolation, no macro leakage. Slowly entering practice. |

### Key Library Additions

- **`std::format`** — type-safe string formatting inspired by Python's `{}`. Predecessor of `std::print` (C++23).
- **`std::span`** — a non-owning view over a contiguous sequence. Replaces `(T*, size_t)` in APIs.
- **`std::jthread`** — a RAII wrapper around `std::thread` with automatic join and cancellation via `stop_token`.
- **`std::atomic_ref`** — atomic access to a non-atomic object (useful for arrays).
- **`std::bit_cast`** — type-punning without UB: bitwise reinterpretation of a value.
- **Three-way comparison (`<=>`)** — one operator instead of six. `= default` auto-generates all comparisons.
- **`std::latch`, `std::barrier`, `std::semaphore`** — synchronization primitives for coordinating threads.

---

## How C++23 Builds on C++20

C++23 is not a revolution — it's a completion of what C++20 laid the groundwork for.

### std::generator (on top of coroutines)

C++20 gave the coroutine mechanism but no ready-made generator type. C++23 fixed that:

```cpp
// C++23
#include <generator>
std::generator<int> fibonacci() {
    int a = 0, b = 1;
    while (true) {
        co_yield a;
        auto tmp = a + b; a = b; b = tmp;
    }
}
```

### ranges::to and New Views

C++20 introduced ranges. C++23 added **`ranges::to<Container>()`** to materialize lazy pipelines into concrete containers, plus 14 new views: `std::views::zip`, `std::views::enumerate`, `std::views::chunk`, `std::views::slide`, `std::views::cartesian_product`, and more.

### std::expected

A type-safe way to return either a value or an error — without exceptions:

```cpp
// C++23
std::expected<int, std::string> parse(std::string_view s);
auto result = parse("42");
if (result) std::cout << *result;  // 42
else        std::cout << result.error();
```

### std::print

C++20 gave `std::format`. C++23 added `std::print` and `std::println` — direct output without `std::cout <<`.

---

## What C++26 Adds on Top of C++23

### Static Reflection (P2996)

Compile-time type introspection. Read struct fields, generate code, build metadata — all via the `^^` and `[: :]` syntax. A flagship feature comparable in scale to concepts.

### Contracts (P2900)

`pre`, `post`, `contract_assert` — invariant checking built into the language. After 20 years of discussion, finally in the standard.

### std::execution (P2300 / Senders-Receivers)

A standard library for structured asynchrony and parallelism. Built on top of C++20 coroutines.

### Pack Indexing (P2662)

Direct access to parameter pack elements: `Args...[0]`, `Args...[N]`.

---

## Compiler Support

C++20 is broadly supported by all three major compilers from these versions:

| Compiler | Minimum version for C++20 | Flag |
|----------|---------------------------|------|
| GCC | 10 (modules — 11+) | `-std=c++20` |
| Clang | 10 (modules — 15+) | `-std=c++20` |
| MSVC | 19.28 (VS 2019 16.8) | `/std:c++20` |

Practical guidance: GCC 12 / Clang 14 / MSVC 19.32 give good coverage of all four flagship features without unpleasant surprises.

Modules are the most delayed piece: as of 2024, the tooling (CMake, clangd, MSVC) has finally reached an acceptable state, but practical adoption still lags.

---

## Order of Study for Practitioners

If you're just starting to apply C++20 in real projects, a sensible sequence:

1. **Concepts** — immediately valuable in any template code. Low barrier to entry.
2. **Ranges** — replace old `std::sort`/`std::find_if` with range algorithms; instantly more readable.
3. **`std::format`** — eliminate `sprintf`, `ostringstream`, and string concatenation with `+`.
4. **`std::span`**, **`<=>` operator**, **`std::jthread`** — as you encounter them in code.
5. **Coroutines** — when you need async; use through a library (cppcoro, Asio, C++23 `generator`).
6. **Modules** — when the tooling in your project is mature enough.

---

## Course Wrap-Up

C++20 didn't just add new keywords to the language — it shifted the perspective on several fundamental tasks:

- Template programming is now **expressive** (concepts).
- Working with sequences is now **declarative** (ranges).
- Async code is now **structured** (coroutines → C++23 execution → C++26 std::execution).
- Invariants are now **built in** (C++26 contracts).
- Metaprogramming is now **safe** (C++26 reflection).

The path from C++14 to C++26 is a single coherent evolution — not a collection of unrelated additions.
